question: A query filtering on WHERE status = 'active' AND created_at > '2024-01-01' runs slowly despite having separate indexes on both status and created_at. What index design would fix this, and why do two single-column indexes not help as much as expected?
answer: A composite B-tree index on (status, created_at) navigates to the first active row then scans forward sequentially, reading only matching entries. Two single-column indexes force a bitmap scan: PostgreSQL builds a bitmap from each index, ANDs them, then fetches rows by physical location — random I/O a sequential scan never pays. Bitmap intersection sorts row locations and passes over data twice. The trade-off: the composite index does not help queries filtering only on created_at.
explanation:
## How It Works

A composite B-tree index stores tuples of column values in sorted order. The index sorts by the first column, then within each first-column value, by the second column, and so on. This ordering directly supports queries using the **leftmost prefix**: equality conditions on leading columns followed by a range condition on the next column.

When a query has `WHERE status = 'active' AND created_at > '2024-01-01'`, a composite index on `(status, created_at)` lets the database navigate the B-tree to the first row matching both conditions, then scan forward sequentially through contiguous index pages. Every page read is relevant — no wasted I/O.

With two single-column indexes, the database has two options. It can use one index to find all matching row locations, then visit each row's heap page to check the other column — random I/O on every row. Or it can use a **bitmap scan**: build a bitmap of page locations from the status index, build another from the created_at index, intersect them with a BitmapAnd operation, then fetch only the surviving rows. This avoids checking every row but still requires sorting row locations and performing random heap accesses.

## Code Example

```sql
-- Slow setup: two separate indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_status ON orders (status);
CREATE INDEX idx_created ON orders (created_at);

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'active' AND created_at > '2024-01-01';
--  Bitmap Heap Scan on orders  (cost=4872.31..10234.19 rows=84500 width=120)
--    Recheck Cond: ((status = 'active') AND (created_at > '2024-01-01'))
--    ->  BitmapAnd
--          ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_status  (cost=0.00..1421.45 rows=120000 width=0)
--          ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_created (cost=0.00..3120.80 rows=240000 width=0)
--  Planning Time: 1.2 ms
--  Execution Time: 892.4 ms

-- Fast setup: composite index
CREATE INDEX idx_status_created ON orders (status, created_at);

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'active' AND created_at > '2024-01-01';
--  Index Scan using idx_status_created on orders  (cost=0.42..3450.10 rows=84500 width=120)
--    Index Cond: ((status = 'active') AND (created_at > '2024-01-01'))
--  Planning Time: 0.8 ms
--  Execution Time: 134.2 ms
```

The composite index reduces execution time from ~890ms to ~134ms — a 6.6x improvement — by eliminating the bitmap build phase and replacing random heap fetches with sequential index reads.

## Key Points

- **Leftmost prefix rule**: an index on (a, b, c) supports queries on a, (a,b), and (a,b,c) but not on b alone or (b,c) alone
- **Equality before range**: columns with `=` conditions go first; range columns (`>`, `<`, `BETWEEN`) go last because a range scan in one column means subsequent columns are not sorted
- **Bitmap scan is not free**: building two bitmaps and intersecting them requires sorting row locations and random heap access — always slower than a single index scan
- **Insert overhead**: each additional index increases write latency; composite indexes are not always the answer for write-heavy workloads

## Common Mistake

Adding individual indexes on every column that appears in a WHERE clause and expecting the database to use all of them. Developers assume the query planner will perfectly combine indexes, but bitmap intersection is a fallback, not a primary strategy. The cost comes from two sources: building each bitmap requires a full index scan, and the BitmapAnd produces unordered page locations, forcing random heap accesses. The mistake is thinking indexes compose freely — they do not. Each query needs a purposeful index strategy, not a collection of single-column indexes.

## Interview Tip

The interviewer is testing whether you understand B-tree index structure at the storage level. A good answer recommends a composite index. A great answer explains **why** the composite index is faster: sequential I/O vs random I/O, the bitmap intersection overhead, and the leftmost prefix rule. The best answers also discuss ordering within the composite index — placing the equality column first and the range column last — and acknowledge the trade-off that the composite index does not help queries filtering only on created_at.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  Q[Query: WHERE status = 'active'\nAND created_at > '2024-01-01'] --> D{Index strategy}

  D -->|Two single-column indexes| SA[Bitmap Index Scan\non idx_status]
  D -->|Two single-column indexes| CA[Bitmap Index Scan\non idx_created]
  SA --> BV[Build Bitmap of\nmatching page locations]
  CA --> CV[Build Bitmap of\nmatching page locations]
  BV --> AND[BitmapAND\nintersect both bitmaps\nsort row locations]
  CV --> AND
  AND --> FH[Heap Fetch\nrandom I/O per row]
  FH --> SLOW[❌ 892 ms\ntwo passes + random I/O]

  D -->|Composite index\n(status, created_at)| CI[Index Scan\non idx_status_created]
  CI --> SCAN[Sequential scan through\ncontiguous index pages\nonly matching rows read]
  SCAN --> FAST[✅ 134 ms\nsingle sequential pass]
